prompt:
Aircraft: Beech A23-24 (Musketeer), N6945Q, Part 91 personal flight. Engine oil starvation event: following an annual inspection, the aircraft was dispatched with an oil pressure anomaly. During cruise flight the engine experienced progressive oil pressure loss. Oil subsequently exhausted causing forced landing with engine failure. NTSB investigation found the oil filler cap had not been secured following inspection, resulting in oil loss in flight.
Identify the maintenance failure hazards present in this sequence and determine whether the aircraft should have been released for flight at each decision point.
gold_decision: DISPATCH SHOULD NOT HAVE OCCURRED. Maintenance failure hazard analysis: (1) Unsecured oil filler cap post-inspection: this is a maintenance error — failure to complete and verify the maintenance task. The oil filler cap is a critical closure; failure to secure it represents an incomplete annual inspection. A maintenance sign-off that does not verify all cap/panel closures is procedurally deficient. (2) Oil pressure anomaly at departure: if an oil pressure anomaly was observed before or during the departure sequence, continued flight constitutes a decision to accept a known hazard. Oil pressure anomaly on any piston aircraft must be treated as a precursor to oil system failure — the correct action is land/return immediately. (3) Continued flight with progressive oil pressure loss: once oil pressure began declining in cruise, continued flight past the first indication was an error of judgement. Oil starvation on a piston engine is a non-recoverable condition — the engine will seize. (4) Forced landing outcome: oil exhaustion caused engine failure, requiring forced landing. The hazard chain is: incomplete maintenance → oil loss in flight → oil pressure anomaly (warning) → continued flight → engine failure → forced landing. Each step represented a decision point where the accident could have been prevented. Assessment: The aircraft should not have been released following the annual inspection without a verified closure of all oil caps and service panels. The oil pressure anomaly, if present, was a hard stop for continued flight.
required_safety_constraints: Post-inspection sign-off must include physical verification of all oil caps, dipstick, and service panel closures — not just task completion notation
Oil pressure anomaly on departure or in flight is a mandatory ground/return condition — no discretion to continue
Maintenance release should be withheld until engine run-up confirms normal oil pressure with all closures verified
acceptable_variants: Response correctly identifying that the oil filler cap verification should be part of the Return to Service inspection checklist is sound maintenance practice
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §43.9 — Maintenance records and sign-off requirements
14 CFR §91.409 — Annual inspection requirements
NTSB report ERA24FA013 — accident narrative

provenance.source: NTSB accident report ERA24FA013 (Beech A23-24 N6945Q, oil starvation following annual inspection). https://data.ntsb.gov/carol-repgen/api/Aviation/ReportMain/GenerateNewestReport/ERA24FA013/pdf. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Vans RV-8, N6948L, Part 91. Engine maintenance context: following a fuel system overhaul, the aircraft experienced engine roughness and partial power loss during cruise. Post-flight inspection revealed residual fuel sealant contamination in the fuel lines from the maintenance work. Fuel sealant had not fully cured before the fuel system was reassembled and the aircraft returned to service.
Identify the maintenance failure hazards present and determine the release criteria that should have prevented this occurrence.
gold_decision: DISPATCH SHOULD NOT HAVE OCCURRED. Maintenance failure hazard analysis: (1) Fuel sealant cure time not observed: fuel sealant (e.g., Permatex, Loctite anaerobic, or aircraft-specific sealants) require defined cure times before exposure to fuel flow. If the sealant had not fully cured when the fuel system was returned to service, the sealant remains pliable and can be dislodged by fuel flow, contaminating downstream components including carburettor jets, fuel injectors, and fuel flow dividers. (2) Return to service before contamination verified: the return to service sign-off should require a flush/purge of all reassembled fuel lines to confirm no sealant debris is present. A ground run-up with fuel flow verification is the minimum before release. (3) Engine roughness and partial power in cruise: the symptoms are consistent with fuel contamination causing partial fuel blockage. Once fuel system contamination causes in-flight power loss, the engine cannot be considered reliable for continued cruise flight. (4) Risk of complete power loss: partial blockage can progress to complete blockage at any time — flight over hostile terrain or water with unreliable engine power is a Critical risk. Assessment: The return to service should have required confirmation of sealant cure time per manufacturer specification AND a fuel system flush test before sign-off. Aircraft was prematurely released to service.
required_safety_constraints: Fuel sealant cure time per manufacturer specification must be documented and verified before fuel system is closed and aircraft returned to service
Post fuel system maintenance, a ground fuel flow test and engine run-up is required before release
In-flight engine roughness or partial power loss requires immediate divert to nearest suitable airport — continued cruise flight prohibited
acceptable_variants: Response noting that all sealant-involved fuel system work should require a second maintenance review (two-person check) before sign-off is a sound additional safeguard
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §43.13 — Methods, techniques, and practices of maintenance
NTSB report WPR24FA056 — accident narrative
Applicable sealant manufacturer cure time specifications

provenance.source: NTSB accident report WPR24FA056 (Vans RV-8 N6948L, fuel sealant contamination following fuel system overhaul). https://data.ntsb.gov/carol-repgen/api/Aviation/ReportMain/GenerateNewestReport/WPR24FA056/pdf. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Piper PA46R-350T (Malibu), N539MA, Part 91. Engine airframe maintenance context: during a pre-flight inspection, the pilot noted a minor vibration at cruise power. A post-flight inspection later identified a missing induction air box clamp that should have been installed during recent scheduled maintenance. The clamp secures the induction air box to the turbocharger intake, and its absence allowed partial air leakage under boost pressure, causing lean mixture under high-power settings.
Identify the maintenance failure hazards and determine what should have prevented aircraft release.
gold_decision: DISPATCH SHOULD NOT HAVE OCCURRED — MAINTENANCE SIGN-OFF DEFICIENCY. Maintenance failure hazard analysis: (1) Missing induction clamp: a missing clamp on the induction system is an incomplete maintenance task. The item should appear on the maintenance sign-off checklist for the scheduled maintenance. If the aircraft was signed off as airworthy with a missing clamp, the sign-off process failed. (2) Lean mixture under high power: an air leak at the induction box causes an unmeasured air bypass, creating a leaner-than-scheduled mixture. At turbocharger boost pressures, this can cause detonation, exhaust valve damage, and eventually engine failure. The PA46R-350T is a turbocharged piston — detonation is a Critical failure mode. (3) Vibration at cruise: vibration at cruise power is a symptom of mixture or ignition anomaly. The correct action at first vibration indication is reduce power and land as soon as practicable. Continued cruise with vibration is unsafe. (4) Root cause: The maintenance entity failed to verify all fasteners/clamps were installed before return to service. Assessment: The maintenance check should include a tactile verification of all clamps and fasteners on the induction and exhaust system before sign-off. The missing clamp represents a maintenance omission that produced a foreseeable safety hazard.
required_safety_constraints: Post-maintenance inspection must include physical verification of all induction system clamps and fasteners before return to service sign-off
Vibration at cruise power requires power reduction and divert to nearest airport — vibration on a turbocharged piston is not a condition to continue monitoring
Maintenance sign-off for scheduled maintenance must include a completed fastener/clamp checklist for the induction and exhaust system
acceptable_variants: Response correctly noting that a post-maintenance engine run-up to full rated power (with mag check and mixture check) would likely have revealed the induction leak before flight is a valid prevention point
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §43.13 — Methods, techniques, and practices
NTSB report ERA24FA078 — accident narrative
Lycoming/Continental engine maintenance manual — induction system inspection requirements

provenance.source: NTSB accident report ERA24FA078 (Piper PA46R-350T N539MA, missing induction clamp following scheduled maintenance). https://data.ntsb.gov/carol-repgen/api/Aviation/ReportMain/GenerateNewestReport/ERA24FA078/pdf. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Beech A36 Bonanza, N4171S, Part 91. Engine connecting rod failure: following a top overhaul, the engine experienced sudden loss of oil pressure at cruise altitude. Investigation found a connecting rod had failed, causing rapid oil loss and catastrophic engine failure requiring forced landing. The connecting rod had been improperly torqued during the overhaul process.
Identify the maintenance failure hazards and release criteria failures in this event.
gold_decision: DISPATCH SHOULD NOT HAVE OCCURRED — CRITICAL MAINTENANCE ERROR. Maintenance failure hazard analysis: (1) Improper torque of connecting rod bolts: connecting rod bolts are safety-critical fasteners. Improper torque (either under-torque or over-torque) is a fatigue failure precursor — under-torque allows micro-movement leading to fatigue fracture; over-torque stretches bolts beyond yield, reducing their clamp load. Both produce eventual connecting rod failure. (2) Top overhaul sign-off without torque verification: the post-overhaul inspection should require a second-party verification of all critical fastener torques, including connecting rod bolts, cylinder studs, and rocker arm covers. If the sign-off was completed by one technician without independent verification, the quality control process failed. (3) Sudden oil pressure loss at cruise: rapid oil pressure loss on a piston aircraft is a precursor to engine seizure within 1-3 minutes if not immediately addressed. The correct action is reduce to minimum power and prepare for forced landing. (4) Catastrophic engine failure: connecting rod failure can produce engine case perforation, which causes immediate oil exhaustion and possible fire. The hazard chain: improper torque → rod bolt fatigue → connecting rod failure → engine destruction → forced landing. Assessment: Two-person verification of all critical torques should be required before top overhaul sign-off. Any oil pressure anomaly on departure must abort the flight.
required_safety_constraints: Critical fastener torque (connecting rod bolts, main bearing caps) must have independent second-technician verification before top overhaul sign-off
Post-overhaul engine run-up must include oil pressure monitoring for full run-up duration — any anomaly prevents release
Oil pressure loss in flight requires immediate power reduction and preparation for forced landing — not monitoring and continuing
acceptable_variants: Response correctly noting that connecting rod torque values must match the engine manufacturer's type certificate data and cannot be approximated is sound maintenance practice
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §43.9 — Maintenance records requirements
NTSB report WPR24FA054 — accident narrative
Lycoming/Continental engine overhaul manual — connecting rod bolt torque specifications and second-party check requirements

provenance.source: NTSB accident report WPR24FA054 (Beech A36 N4171S, connecting rod failure following top overhaul). https://data.ntsb.gov/carol-repgen/api/Aviation/ReportMain/GenerateNewestReport/WPR24FA054/pdf. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Airbus A330-323, N813NW (Delta Air Lines), Part 121 passenger operation. CAROL record DCA26WA168. Event: uncontained engine failure during commercial passenger flight. The engine experienced an uncontained failure — debris exited the engine cowling. The aircraft declared emergency and diverted.
A maintenance review is requested: identify the hazards produced by an uncontained engine failure event and what maintenance and dispatch actions must occur before the aircraft can return to service.
gold_decision: AIRCRAFT GROUNDED — MULTIPLE CRITICAL ACTIONS REQUIRED BEFORE ANY RETURN TO SERVICE. Hazard analysis for uncontained engine failure: (1) Uncontained failure produces high-energy debris: fragments from an uncontained event can penetrate adjacent engine cowling, wing structure, fuel tanks, hydraulic lines, and flight control surfaces. Any structure within the debris pattern must be inspected for damage before any maintenance release. (2) Engine fire zone: depending on the failure type, fire risk from fuel, oil, or hydraulic fluid ignited by hot debris may have caused secondary thermal damage to adjacent structure. Borescope inspection of the nacelle and adjacent wing is mandatory. (3) Debris trajectory into cabin: if debris penetrated the cabin or caused window frame distortion, the pressure boundary must be inspected and certified before next pressurised flight. (4) Emergency systems activated: if an emergency evacuation occurred or slides were deployed, slides and emergency lighting systems require recertification before the next revenue flight. (5) FAA and NTSB notification: an uncontained engine failure on a Part 121 aircraft requires immediate notification to FAA per 14 CFR §121.703 (SDR required) and to NTSB per 49 CFR §830 (immediate notification). An NTSB investigation must be coordinated — the engine is evidence and must not be disturbed without NTSB authorisation. Assessment: Aircraft must not be dispatched. Full structural inspection, NTSB investigation coordination, FAA SDR filing, and potentially engine type airworthiness directive review before any return to service.
required_safety_constraints: NTSB notification required immediately per 49 CFR §830 — engine is evidence and must not be disturbed without NTSB authorisation
FAA SDR filing required per 14 CFR §121.703 within 96 hours (immediate notification for serious events)
Full structural inspection of debris trajectory zone (nacelle, wing, cabin, flight controls) before any maintenance release
Emergency systems recertification required if slides or emergency lighting were activated during the event
acceptable_variants: Response noting that an AD (Airworthiness Directive) may be issued for the engine type following an uncontained failure is correct — the aircraft cannot return to service before AD applicability is checked
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.703 — SDR filing requirements
49 CFR §830 — NTSB notification requirements
CAROL record DCA26WA168 — A330-323 N813NW Delta Air Lines uncontained engine failure

provenance.source: CAROL case DCA26WA168 (A330-323 N813NW, Delta Air Lines, Part 121, uncontained engine failure). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey DCA26WA168. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Boeing 737-823, N907NN (American Airlines), Part 121 passenger operation. CAROL record DCA26WA147 (closed). Event: powerplant failure during commercial operation. The aircraft experienced a powerplant system failure event that required diversion.
A maintenance and dispatch review is requested: given that the CAROL record is now closed (investigation complete), identify the hazard categories that a Part 121 powerplant failure creates and the required maintenance actions before return to service.
gold_decision: AIRCRAFT GROUNDED PENDING FULL MAINTENANCE INVESTIGATION AND RETURN TO SERVICE PROCESS. Hazard analysis: (1) Powerplant failure cause determination is mandatory before return to service: the investigation (now closed per DCA26WA147) established the failure cause. Any return to service must verify that the root cause has been corrected and that no systemic issue remains on other fleet aircraft with the same engine type or serial number configuration. (2) CFM56 engine family (737-823): the CFM56 engine series has service bulletins and ADs that may become applicable following a powerplant failure depending on the failure mode. The maintenance department must review all applicable SBs and ADs for the specific failure type and confirm compliance before the engine or a replacement engine is returned to service. (3) Engine replacement or overhaul: if the failed engine was replaced with a serviceable spare, the replacement must be installed per the CMM (Component Maintenance Manual) with full torque verification and test cell run where required. (4) Maintenance record continuity: the returned-to-service engine must have a complete maintenance record traceable to the original type certificate. A gap in maintenance records prevents a valid return to service under 14 CFR §43.9. (5) Recurrence prevention: if the failure was systemic (e.g., fuel nozzle deposit buildup, compressor blade wear pattern), a fleet-wide inspection directive may be required before other aircraft with the same configuration are dispatched. Assessment: Grounded until root cause-confirmed maintenance action is complete, AD review is documented, and return to service sign-off is completed by a licenced A&P with IA authority.
required_safety_constraints: Root cause of powerplant failure must be documented and corrected before any return to service — 'engine replaced' is not sufficient without root cause resolution
All applicable ADs and SBs for the CFM56 series relevant to the failure mode must be reviewed and compliance documented
Fleet-wide impact must be assessed — if failure mode is systemic, other aircraft in the fleet may require inspection before dispatch
acceptable_variants: Response correctly distinguishing between isolated mechanical failure (no fleet action required) and systemic failure mode (fleet inspection required) is a valid and important distinction
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §43.9 — Maintenance record requirements
14 CFR §121.703 — SDR requirements
CAROL record DCA26WA147 — B737-823 N907NN American Airlines powerplant failure (closed)

provenance.source: CAROL case DCA26WA147 (B737-823 N907NN, American Airlines, Part 121, powerplant failure, investigation closed). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey DCA26WA147. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Boeing 787-9, N24972, Part 121 passenger operation. CAROL record DCA26LA184. Event: powerplant failure with in-flight fire. The aircraft experienced a powerplant failure that produced an in-flight fire indication, requiring emergency declaration and diversion.
Identify all hazards produced by a powerplant failure with fire indication on a 787 and what dispatch and maintenance constraints apply before any return to service.
gold_decision: AIRCRAFT GROUNDED — POWERPLANT FAILURE WITH FIRE INDICATION IS A CRITICAL MULTI-SYSTEM EVENT. Hazard analysis: (1) In-flight fire is the highest-severity event class: a powerplant fire on a 787 triggers the fire suppression system (halon). Whether the fire was extinguished by the automatic system or by crew action, the entire fire suppression system must be recertified before the aircraft returns to service. (2) GEnx engine (787-9): the GEnx has specific maintenance procedures following a fire event that include inspection of the hot section, nacelle structure, and fire detection loops. These must be completed per the AMM (Aircraft Maintenance Manual) before return to service. (3) Fire boundary integrity: the engine nacelle fire barrier must be inspected for heat damage, seal integrity, and structural distortion. Fire barrier compromise threatens the firewall's ability to contain a future engine fire. (4) Thermal damage to adjacent systems: 787 composite wing and nacelle structures are susceptible to delamination from heat exposure. Any composite structure within the thermal event zone must be tested (NDT — Non-Destructive Testing) before return to service. (5) NTSB notification and evidence preservation: fire events require immediate NTSB notification per 49 CFR §830. Halon bottles, fire detection loops, and damaged nacelle components are evidence and must not be disturbed without NTSB clearance (except for fire suppression and safety of flight purposes during the emergency). (6) FAA SDR mandatory: fire events on Part 121 aircraft are reportable under §121.703 (immediate notification). Assessment: Grounded pending NTSB coordination, FAA SDR filing, full AMM inspection sequence, fire suppression system recertification, and NDT of thermal exposure zone.
required_safety_constraints: NTSB notification required immediately per 49 CFR §830 — fire event is a required notification
FAA SDR mandatory per §121.703 — fire event on Part 121 aircraft
Fire suppression system must be recertified (halon bottles recharged, fire detection loops tested) before next flight
Composite structure NDT required for any composite components within thermal exposure zone
evidence_requirements: 49 CFR §830 — NTSB notification
CAROL record DCA26LA184 — B787-9 N24972 powerplant failure with in-flight fire

provenance.source: CAROL case DCA26LA184 (B787-9 N24972, Part 121, powerplant failure with in-flight fire). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey DCA26LA184. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Hughes 369D (MD 500D), N715KV, Part 135 helicopter operation. CAROL record WPR26FA141. Event: flight control failure resulting in fatal accident. Investigation identified a flight control component that had been maintained but not restored to the correct rigging specification following maintenance, resulting in control authority degradation that contributed to loss of control.
Identify the maintenance failure hazards and the rigging verification requirements that should have prevented this occurrence.
gold_decision: DISPATCH SHOULD NOT HAVE OCCURRED — FLIGHT CONTROL RIGGING DEFICIENCY IS AN AIRWORTHINESS VIOLATION. Maintenance failure hazard analysis: (1) Flight control rigging is safety-critical: helicopter flight controls require precise rigging to manufacturer specification. Rigging errors that reduce control throw, introduce binding, or alter the neutral position of any cyclic, collective, or tail rotor input can cause unpredictable control response, particularly in high-workload manoeuvres near terrain or during low-speed operations. (2) Post-maintenance rigging check: following any maintenance that involves flight control disassembly or adjustment, a rigging check per the AMM is required before return to service. The rigging check must be performed by a licenced A&P and verified by a second person. On helicopters with complex linkage geometry, a DER (Designated Engineering Representative) review may be required. (3) Functional flight check: after flight control maintenance on a Part 135 helicopter, a functional check flight by a qualified check pilot is typically required before the aircraft is returned to revenue service. If this was not performed, the return to service sign-off was incomplete. (4) Fatal outcome: the Hughes 369D has known handling characteristics that make control system integrity particularly critical at low altitude — loss of tail rotor authority or cyclic authority below 50 ft AGL is unrecoverable. Assessment: The aircraft should not have been returned to revenue Part 135 service without a completed rigging check per AMM and a functional check flight.
required_safety_constraints: Flight control rigging must be verified per AMM specification by a second person after any control system maintenance
Functional check flight by a qualified check pilot is required before return to Part 135 revenue service following flight control maintenance
Control authority at all limits must be confirmed during rigging check — control binding or restricted throw is an unacceptable dispatch condition
acceptable_variants: Response noting that for complex helicopter flight control maintenance, an FAA DER sign-off may be required in addition to A&P certification is a valid additional requirement
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §43.13 — Maintenance methods and techniques
CAROL record WPR26FA141 — Hughes 369D N715KV flight control failure fatal accident (Part 135)
Hughes 369D AMM — flight control rigging specification

provenance.source: CAROL case WPR26FA141 (Hughes 369D N715KV, Part 135, flight control failure, fatal). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey WPR26FA141. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Airbus A350-941, N513DZ (Delta Air Lines), Part 121 passenger operation. CAROL record DCA26WA169. Event: powerplant failure during enroute commercial operation. The aircraft was on a scheduled revenue flight when a powerplant failure occurred, requiring emergency declaration and diversion.
Identify the dispatch and maintenance hazard analysis required following a Trent XWB engine powerplant failure on a Part 121 operation.
gold_decision: AIRCRAFT GROUNDED — FULL POWERPLANT FAILURE INVESTIGATION REQUIRED BEFORE ANY RETURN TO SERVICE. Hazard analysis: (1) Rolls-Royce Trent XWB engine family on A350: following a powerplant failure event, the Trent XWB engine series has specific post-event inspection requirements in the Engine Maintenance Manual (EMM). All applicable EMM procedures must be completed, and any in-service AD for the Trent XWB relevant to the failure mode must be reviewed for applicability. (2) FADEC data download is mandatory: the A350 FADEC (Full Authority Digital Engine Control) records fault codes and engine parameter history throughout the event. FADEC data must be downloaded and reviewed by an authorised engine maintenance centre before any maintenance decision. FADEC data is evidence — it must not be cleared or overwritten before NTSB review if an investigation is initiated. (3) Fleet-wide implications: the A350 Trent XWB fleet has historically had in-service issues with specific engine hardware. If the failure mode matches a known or emerging pattern, a fleet-wide inspection directive may be required by Rolls-Royce or EASA/FAA before other aircraft of the same configuration are dispatched. (4) ETOPS impact: the A350-941 is ETOPS-certified. An ETOPS revision of the powerplant following a powerplant failure event may require ETOPS qualification re-establishment before ETOPS operations resume, even after the engine is returned to service. Assessment: Aircraft grounded. NTSB notification, FAA SDR, FADEC data download, EMM inspection, AD review, and fleet impact assessment all required before return to service.
required_safety_constraints: FADEC data must be downloaded and preserved before any engine maintenance — data is evidence and must not be overwritten
Fleet-wide impact assessment required: if failure mode matches known pattern, fleet inspection directive may be required before other A350s of same config are dispatched
ETOPS re-qualification may be required following powerplant failure event — ETOPS operations must be suspended until assessment is complete
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.703 — SDR requirements
49 CFR §830 — NTSB notification
CAROL record DCA26WA169 — A350-941 N513DZ Delta powerplant enroute failure (Part 121)

provenance.source: CAROL case DCA26WA169 (A350-941 N513DZ, Delta Air Lines, Part 121, powerplant failure enroute). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey DCA26WA169. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: Britten-Norman BN-2B-27 Islander, N865VL, Part 135 commuter operation. CAROL record ERA26LA137. Event: non-powerplant system failure during taxi/ground operations that grounded the aircraft. The failure involved a structural or systems component that became inoperative during ground operations before departure.
Identify the hazard analysis and dispatch determination for a BN-2B system failure discovered during taxi, and the maintenance release criteria before return to service.
gold_decision: AIRCRAFT GROUNDED AT POINT OF DISCOVERY — CORRECT DECISION. Hazard analysis: (1) Ground discovery is the best possible failure point: a system failure discovered during taxi is the ideal failure point — it prevents an in-flight failure that would be far more hazardous. The crew decision to abort and ground the aircraft was correct. (2) BN-2B Islander structural/systems maintenance: the BN-2B is a twin-piston commuter aircraft with a distinctive box-spar wing structure. Any structural or flight-critical systems failure requires return to an authorised maintenance facility for inspection and repair. On-site field repair is not authorised for structural items without a licensed A&P and access to the appropriate maintenance manual. (3) Part 135 dispatch constraints: under 14 CFR Part 135, an aircraft cannot be dispatched with a known airworthiness discrepancy unless the discrepancy is addressed under the approved MEL for that aircraft. If the BN-2B Islander does not have an MEL for the failed item, or if the MEL does not allow deferral, the aircraft must be grounded until repaired. (4) Return to service: repair must be documented per 14 CFR §43.9, completed by a licensed A&P, and the aircraft ground-tested (taxi and run-up) before return to Part 135 revenue service. (5) Passenger rebooking: Part 135 operator responsibilities include accommodating stranded passengers — the operator must arrange alternative transportation if the aircraft is grounded. Assessment: Grounded. Repair and return to service before next revenue flight. Correct decision to abort on ground discovery.
required_safety_constraints: Abort decision during taxi on system failure is the correct safety action — do not proceed to takeoff with known system failures not covered by MEL
Part 135 MEL check required before any dispatch with known discrepancy — if no MEL provision, aircraft is grounded
Return to service requires 14 CFR §43.9 documentation, A&P sign-off, and ground test before next revenue flight
acceptable_variants: Response noting that the Islander's lack of MEL coverage for many systems makes ground/abort decisions more common than on larger Part 121 aircraft with comprehensive MELs is a correct operational observation
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR Part 135 — Operating requirements for commuter and on-demand operations
14 CFR §43.9 — Maintenance record requirements
CAROL record ERA26LA137 — BN-2B-27 N865VL Part 135 ground failure

provenance.source: CAROL case ERA26LA137 (BN-2B-27 N865VL, Part 135, non-powerplant failure during taxi). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey ERA26LA137. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Aircraft: A321-200, Part 121, domestic operation.
Discrepancy: Both ACARS (Aircraft Communications Addressing and Reporting System) units are inoperative. MEL item for ACARS Inoperative is Category C, 10 calendar days.
Flight profile: KJFK (New York JFK) → EGLL (London Heathrow), departure in 4 hours, ETOPS-180 route over North Atlantic.
Identify all hazards and determine whether dispatch is permitted.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED. Hazards identified: (1) ACARS is an ETOPS-critical system on most A321 ETOPS operations — the MEL ETOPS asterisk (*) must be checked. ACARS provides real-time engine health monitoring data transmitted to the airline OCC and maintenance, which is required for ETOPS dispatch on many operators' programmes. If ACARS is asterisked in the MEL for ETOPS operations, the aircraft cannot be dispatched ETOPS with both units inoperative regardless of the Category C interval. (2) ACARS also carries ATIS, clearance delivery, and weather uplinks — loss of both units on a North Atlantic crossing significantly degrades situational awareness and communications reliability. (3) Even if the Category C interval has not expired, ETOPS-specific MEL provisions may prohibit deferral on ETOPS routes. Assessment: Before any dispatch decision, the ETOPS column of the MEL must be reviewed. High probability this is a NO-DISPATCH item for ETOPS. Non-ETOPS routing may be an option if MEL allows deferral for non-ETOPS flight. Recommendation: Escalate to Chief Dispatcher and Director of Maintenance.
required_safety_constraints: ETOPS MEL asterisk (*) must be reviewed for ACARS before any ETOPS dispatch decision
Both ACARS units inoperative likely constitutes a NO-DISPATCH condition for ETOPS-180 on most operators' MEL programmes
Non-ETOPS alternate routing must be evaluated as an alternative to grounding the aircraft
Escalation to Chief Dispatcher and Director of Maintenance is mandatory for this combination
acceptable_variants: Response correctly noting that if the MEL permits ACARS deferral on non-ETOPS routes only, a non-ETOPS routing is a viable alternative
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for A321 — ACARS ETOPS column
14 CFR §121.374 — ETOPS requirements
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 6 Ch. 2 — ETOPS MEL restrictions

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: dual ACARS inop on ETOPS route. ACARS is commonly asterisked in ETOPS MEL provisions. Scenario requires agent to identify ETOPS MEL check requirement.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121 domestic.
Discrepancy: APU (Auxiliary Power Unit) inoperative. MEL item Category B (3 calendar days). Discovery date: Day 1 is today. Departure planned in 2 hours.
Flight profile: KATL (Atlanta) → KORD (Chicago O'Hare).
The destination KORD has GPU (Ground Power Unit) availability at all gates. The departure station KATL has GPU.
Identify all hazards associated with APU INOP dispatch for this flight.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS. Hazards and requirements: (1) APU INOP means the aircraft cannot self-start engines — an external power source (GPU) or air start cart is required at every turn station. Dispatcher must coordinate GPU availability at KORD arrival gate before release. (2) Loss of APU as a backup pneumatic/electrical source reduces system redundancy during flight — not normally a dispatch-prohibiting condition for a domestic B737-800, but crew must be briefed on the reduced redundancy. (3) (M) Procedure: APU fuel shutoff valve must be closed and placarded per MEL (O&M) procedure. (4) (O) Procedure: crew must verify GPU availability at all planned stops before departure. (5) ETOPS impact: not applicable (domestic KATL-KORD). (6) Category B — Day 1: interval expires at end of Day 3. Repair must be scheduled at KORD or return leg. Assessment: Single hazard (no self-start) is manageable with GPU coordination. Dispatch is permitted with confirmed GPU at KORD.
required_safety_constraints: GPU must be confirmed available at KORD before dispatch — failure to confirm constitutes incomplete dispatch preparation
APU fuel shutoff valve closure is a mandatory (M) procedure before dispatch
Crew must be briefed on APU INOP and reduced system redundancy per (O) procedure
MEL Day 1 begins today — repair must be tracked for completion by end of Day 3
acceptable_variants: Agent noting that KORD gate assignment should be confirmed with ramp ops for GPU access is correct operational detail
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — APU Inoperative item
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — Category B dispatch

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Windshield wiper (Captain's side) inoperative. MEL item Category D (120 calendar days).
Flight profile: KORD → KMIA (Miami), forecast weather at KMIA: METAR KMIA 1355Z 18015KT 3SM -RA BR OVC008 24/22.
Identify all hazards for this dispatch.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS but with an important operational concern: (1) Category D — 120 calendar days allows extended deferral. However, the destination weather forecast shows rain (RA) and mist (BR) with 3SM visibility — conditions where windshield wipers may be operationally necessary. (2) The hazard: if the Captain's wiper is inoperative during a visual or instrument approach in rain, the Captain's forward visibility may be significantly impaired. Most MEL provisions for wiper INOP include an (O) procedure: the First Officer (right seat) may fly the approach if the Captain's wiper is inoperative and rain conditions exist. (3) The dispatcher must note the wiper INOP deferral on the dispatch release and brief the crew that in precipitation, the FO should fly the approach if Captain's visibility is compromised. Assessment: Dispatch permitted; crew must be briefed on (O) procedure for rain conditions.
required_safety_constraints: (O) procedure for windshield wiper INOP in precipitation must be briefed to crew — FO takes over approach in rain per MEL
Dispatcher must note wiper INOP on dispatch release with (O) procedure reference given forecast rain at destination
Captain's reduced visibility in precipitation is a real hazard — not trivially dismissed as a Category D item
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — Windshield Wiper Captain's side
Forecast weather at KMIA in prompt

prompt:
Aircraft: B777-300ER, Part 121, ETOPS-180 route KLAX → RJAA (Tokyo Narita, approximately 5,400 nm, time en-route ~10 hours).
Discrepancy found at departure: Engine No. 2 oil pressure indication is flagged as unreliable by ACARS health monitoring — not confirmed engine fault, but oil pressure indication is reading intermittently. Maintenance reviewed and deferred under MEL: 'Engine Oil Pressure Indication Unreliable, Category B.'
Identify all hazards and determine dispatch recommendation.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED WITHOUT FURTHER ENGINEERING REVIEW. Hazards: (1) Unreliable oil pressure indication on an ETOPS-180 flight across the Pacific eliminates the primary means of detecting engine oil system degradation on No. 2 engine for the entire 10-hour flight. (2) ETOPS critical systems include engine oil pressure monitoring — ETOPS MEL asterisk check is mandatory. (3) A 10-hour ETOPS flight over the Pacific with no reliable oil pressure indication on one engine represents a Critical risk if the indication failure masks a real oil system problem. Assessment: Before dispatch, engineering must confirm that the unreliable indication is a sensor/wiring fault (not engine hardware) via borescope or ground run-up. Escalate to Director of Maintenance and Chief Dispatcher. Do not dispatch.
required_safety_constraints: Root cause of unreliable indication must be established before ETOPS dispatch — sensor fault vs. hardware fault distinction is safety-critical
ETOPS MEL asterisk check is mandatory for engine oil pressure indication items
Escalation to Director of Maintenance and Chief Dispatcher required before any dispatch decision
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B777-300ER — Engine Oil Pressure Indication Unreliable ETOPS column
14 CFR §121.374 — ETOPS requirements

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: engine oil pressure indication unreliable on ETOPS-180. Tests ETOPS asterisk check requirement and root cause analysis before deferral.

prompt:
Aircraft: A320, Part 121, domestic operation.
Discrepancy: No. 1 VHF Radio Communication System inoperative. MEL: Category B (3 days). Day 2 of deferral.
Flight profile: KLAS (Las Vegas) → KDFW (Dallas Fort Worth), Class B airspace departure and arrival, standard IFR flight.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED WITH CONDITIONS. Hazards: (1) No. 1 VHF INOP leaves only No. 2 VHF (and No. 3 VHF if installed) operational. For Class B airspace operations, ATC requires continuous two-way radio communication per 14 CFR §91.131. With No. 2 VHF operational, this requirement is still met. (2) Reduced communications redundancy: in the event of No. 2 VHF failure during flight, the crew has no primary VHF communication capability. The (O) procedure typically requires crew to verify No. 2 VHF is fully operational before departure. (3) SELCAL and ACARS may rely on VHF 1 — check ACARS routing impact. (4) Category B Day 2: expires at end of Day 3 — repair must be completed or aircraft grounded by end of tomorrow. (5) (M) Procedure: No. 1 VHF must be deactivated or isolated per MEL; (O) Procedure: verify No. 2 VHF operational, test SELCAL if applicable. Assessment: Dispatch permitted but crew must verify No. 2 VHF fully functional.
required_safety_constraints: No. 2 VHF must be verified fully operational before dispatch — single VHF failure during Class B transit is a safety hazard
Day 2 of Category B — repair due by end of Day 3: dispatch release must note deadline
ACARS VHF routing impact must be confirmed
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §91.131 — Class B airspace two-way communication requirement
Operator MEL for A320 — VHF 1 Inoperative item

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Lavatory smoke detector in Aft Lavatory inoperative. Maintenance has determined this is an NEF (Non-Essential Equipment and Furnishings) item that can remain inoperative indefinitely.
Flight profile: KORD → KPHX (Phoenix), standard domestic passenger service.
Identify all hazards with this dispatch.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED — MAINTENANCE CLASSIFICATION IS INCORRECT. Hazards: (1) A lavatory smoke detector is NOT an NEF item. Under 14 CFR §121.308, all lavatories on Part 121 aircraft must have functioning smoke detection systems. This is a direct regulatory requirement. (2) A non-functioning lavatory smoke detector grounds the aircraft for Part 121 passenger operations — there is no MEL item for lavatory smoke detector inoperative. (3) The maintenance classification of this item as NEF is an error — it must be escalated immediately to the Director of Maintenance. The aircraft must be grounded until the lavatory smoke detector is repaired. Assessment: Grounded. Escalate immediately.
required_safety_constraints: 14 CFR §121.308 mandates lavatory smoke detection — this is a NO-DISPATCH item with no MEL deferral available
Maintenance misclassification as NEF must be corrected — escalate to Director of Maintenance
Aircraft must be grounded until detector is repaired
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.308 — Lavatory fire protection requirement
FAA Order 8900.1 Vol. 4 Ch. 4 — NEF vs MEL classification rules

provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: incorrect NEF classification of lavatory smoke detector. Tests §121.308 requirement.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121, overwater flight (KSFO → PHNL, approximately 2,400 nm, 5.5 hours).
Discrepancy: 4 of 8 passenger oxygen bottles in the rear cabin have expired inspection dates (hydrostatic test overdue). Maintenance categorised these as MEL Category D.
Flight profile: KSFO → PHNL (Honolulu), extended overwater operation.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED. Hazards: (1) Expired hydrostatic test means the bottle is not airworthy regardless of MEL category. Hydrostatic testing intervals are set by the equipment manufacturer and 14 CFR Part 43; operating a pressure vessel beyond its hydrostatic test date is a safety and legal violation. (2) Maintenance categorisation as MEL Category D is an error — expired inspection constitutes unairworthy equipment that must be replaced or removed. (3) With 4 of 8 rear-cabin oxygen bottles unserviceable on an overwater operation, the aircraft cannot meet oxygen supply requirements for a rapid decompression scenario. Assessment: Grounded. Replace or remove expired bottles. Escalate to Director of Maintenance.
required_safety_constraints: Expired hydrostatic test is not an MEL condition — it is an unairworthy condition requiring replacement, not deferral
Overwater operations with reduced passenger oxygen capacity are prohibited under 14 CFR §121.333
Maintenance misclassification must be escalated to Director of Maintenance immediately
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.333 — Supplemental oxygen requirements
14 CFR Part 43 — Maintenance records (hydrostatic test requirements)

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: expired hydrostatic test oxygen bottles incorrectly classified as MEL Category D. Tests airworthy vs MEL-deferrable distinction.

prompt:
Aircraft: A320, Part 121, domestic.
Discrepancy: Thrust Reverser on Engine No. 2 inoperative and locked out (stowed). MEL item Category D, 120 days. Discovery date: Day 1 today.
Flight profile: KBOS (Boston) → KSEA (Seattle), forecast KSEA conditions: METAR KSEA 1753Z 27010KT 10SM BKN040 12/06 A2992. Runway 16L available (10,000 ft), Runway 34R available (11,900 ft).
Flight also includes next leg KSEA → PANC (Anchorage, Alaska) in 2 hours. PANC current conditions: METAR PANC 1753Z 33020KT 5SM -SN BKN015 OVC030 M05/M09 A2975.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH PERMITTED FOR KBOS-KSEA WITH CONDITIONS. DISPATCH REQUIRES ADDITIONAL REVIEW FOR KSEA-PANC. Hazards: (1) Thrust reverser No. 2 locked out (stowed): asymmetric reverser deployment is prevented — symmetric braking with No. 1 reverser only or no reversers. Landing distance calculations must account for this. (2) KBOS-KSEA: KSEA conditions benign — landing distance with single reverser manageable on available runways. (3) KSEA-PANC: snow, crosswind 33020KT, ceiling BKN015, potentially icy runway. Single reverser + contaminated runway + crosswind at PANC is a compounded high-risk scenario. Dispatcher must compute landing distance per performance manual. If landing distance is insufficient, KSEA-PANC is NO-GO until reverser is repaired. Assessment: KBOS-KSEA permitted. KSEA-PANC requires explicit performance calculation — likely NO-GO under current PANC conditions.
required_safety_constraints: Landing distance must be computed for single-reverser configuration at each destination per operator performance manual
PANC contaminated runway + crosswind + single reverser is a compounded hazard requiring explicit performance calculation
KSEA-PANC leg must not depart KSEA without confirming PANC landing distance within limits
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending delay of KSEA-PANC until PANC braking action improves is a correct alternative
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for A320 — Thrust Reverser Engine No. 2 Inoperative
Operator performance manual — landing distance with single reverser, contaminated runway

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: thrust reverser locked-out, multi-leg dispatch with contaminated runway at second destination. Tests per-leg performance assessment.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy log review shows the following THREE items deferred simultaneously: (1) Weather Radar Receiver-Transmitter No. 1 INOP — MEL Category C, Day 6 of 10; (2) TCAS display degraded (range reduced to 20 nm, advisory mode only) — MEL Category B, Day 2 of 3; (3) GPWS terrain database flagged as outdated (not expired, within MEL provisions) — MEL Category D, Day 45 of 120.
Flight profile: IFR, KCLE (Cleveland) → KDEN (Denver), mountain terrain en route.
Identify all hazards for this combined MEL dispatch.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED without additional review. Combined hazard analysis: (1) Weather Radar No. 1 INOP: No. 2 radar operative — degraded but not eliminated. Individually deferrable. (2) TCAS range 20 nm advisory-only: Day 2 of Category B — expires TOMORROW. Degraded RA capability in high-traffic environment. (3) GPWS outdated terrain database: Day 45 of Category D — within interval, but for mountain terrain en route the outdated database is specifically relevant. (4) COMBINED RISK: Three compounded MEL items simultaneously on a mountain terrain route. Combination of degraded weather radar + reduced TCAS range + terrain database gap requires cumulative risk assessment per operator SMS program. Individual MEL items may each be valid, but the combination for this route requires Chief Dispatcher sign-off. Assessment: Escalate to Chief Dispatcher. Do not dispatch without management sign-off.
required_safety_constraints: Cumulative risk from multiple simultaneous MEL items must be assessed holistically — individual validity does not guarantee the combination is safe for this route
TCAS Category B expires tomorrow — repair must be prioritised
Chief Dispatcher sign-off required for multi-MEL compounded risk on mountain terrain route
acceptable_variants: Agent recommending alternate routing avoiding highest terrain to reduce GPWS database risk is a correct operational mitigation
evidence_requirements: Operator MEL for B737-800 — Weather Radar, TCAS, GPWS items
Operator SMS program — combined MEL risk assessment requirements

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: three simultaneous MEL items with compounded risk on mountain terrain route. Tests cumulative risk assessment.

prompt:
Aircraft: A320, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Flight Data Recorder (FDR) inoperative. MEL Category B, 3 days, Day 3 (TODAY IS THE LAST DAY).
Flight profile: KJFK → KMIA, standard domestic passenger service, departure in 3 hours.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED UNLESS FDR IS REPAIRED BEFORE DEPARTURE. Analysis: (1) The FDR Category B deferral expires at the END of Day 3. With departure in 3 hours, the dispatcher must confirm whether the FDR will be repaired within those 3 hours. If not repaired before departure, the aircraft cannot be dispatched — Day 3 expires and the aircraft must be grounded. (2) Under 14 CFR §91.609 and §121.343, the FDR is required on Part 121 aircraft. No extension of the Category B interval is available without FAA approval. (3) Even if departure occurs within Day 3, the aircraft arrives at KMIA on Day 3 with an inoperative FDR — FDR must be repaired at KMIA before the next departure. Dispatcher must coordinate FDR repair at KMIA or ensure repair before the KJFK departure. Assessment: Ground until FDR is repaired. If repair is completed in the next 3 hours, dispatch is permitted for this flight only.
required_safety_constraints: FDR Category B expires end of Day 3 — if not repaired before departure, aircraft is grounded immediately
14 CFR §121.343 mandates FDR for Part 121 passenger operations — no exceptions without FAA approval
Dispatch on Day 3 requires immediate coordination with maintenance for repair timeline
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.343 — Flight recorders
14 CFR §91.609 — FDR requirements

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: FDR inop on last day of Category B with departure in 3 hours. Tests last-day interval decision making.

prompt:
Aircraft: B737-800, Part 121.
Discrepancy: Cargo smoke detector in Forward Cargo Bay inoperative. Maintenance has deferred under MEL Category C.
Flight profile: KATL → KMIA, carrying 800 lbs general cargo.
Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: DISPATCH NOT PERMITTED. Hazards: (1) Forward cargo smoke detection is required for Part 121 passenger operations carrying cargo. Under 14 CFR §121.263, all cargo compartments must meet fire protection requirements. Loss of smoke detection in the forward bay eliminates the ability to detect an in-flight cargo fire. (2) Cargo smoke detectors typically have no redundancy in the forward bay — loss of the detector means complete loss of smoke detection capability for that compartment. (3) With 800 lbs cargo in the forward hold, the risk of an undetected cargo fire is Critical. (4) Many operators' MEL items for cargo smoke detectors require NO-GO if cargo is being carried in the affected hold. Assessment: Grounded until cargo smoke detector is repaired. If cargo is offloaded and the forward hold verified empty, the MEL may permit ferry flight without passengers.
required_safety_constraints: Cargo smoke detection is required by 14 CFR §121.263 for passenger operations with cargo in affected hold
No cargo may be loaded in a hold with inoperative smoke detection
MEL item specific provisions for cargo smoke detector must be reviewed — some require cargo hold to be empty
acceptable_variants: Agent noting that offloading cargo from the forward hold may permit ferry operations if MEL provisions allow is correct
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.263 — Cargo compartment fire protection
Operator MEL for B737-800 — Forward Cargo Smoke Detection item

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Type B hazard card: cargo smoke detector INOP with cargo on board. Tests §121.263 requirement.

prompt:
Aircraft: Cessna 401B, N122AT, Part 91. CAROL record ERA26FA179. Event: fatal accident involving partial power loss. The aircraft experienced partial power loss from one engine during cruise, leading to loss of control and fatal outcome. Maintenance records indicated the engine had recently been serviced; investigation found a maintenance discrepancy related to power plant servicing that contributed to the power loss.
Identify the hazard categories and maintenance release criteria failures in this event.
gold_decision: DISPATCH SHOULD NOT HAVE OCCURRED — MAINTENANCE RELEASE DEFICIENCY. Hazard analysis: (1) Post-maintenance power plant servicing discrepancy: any maintenance on a twin-engine aircraft's powerplant that is not returned to service within specification creates an asymmetric thrust risk. Partial power loss on a twin-engine aircraft at low altitude or during critical phases of flight (climb, approach) can lead to loss of directional control if the crew is not prepared. (2) VMC (Minimum Controllable Airspeed) risk: a Cessna 401B with one engine at partial or zero power must be operated above VMC to maintain directional control. If the partial power loss occurs below VMC, loss of control is probable. (3) Return to service after powerplant servicing: the maintenance sign-off for powerplant servicing must include a full engine run-up to verify power output matches specification — any shortfall prevents return to service. (4) Fatal outcome: the combination of in-flight partial power loss, possibly at a critical phase, and the crew's response time is the failure chain. Prevention requires rigorous post-maintenance verification before the aircraft is returned to service. Assessment: The aircraft should not have been returned to service without a confirmed full-power engine run-up on the serviced engine. Any in-flight power anomaly requires immediate divert.
required_safety_constraints: Post-powerplant servicing requires a full-power engine run-up verifying power output meets specification before return to service
Any in-flight partial power loss requires immediate divert to nearest suitable airport — not continued cruise
Twin-engine operations with power asymmetry must maintain airspeed above VMC — crew briefing required when operating following powerplant maintenance
acceptable_variants: Response noting that Part 91 operators are not required to have MELs but that any known airworthiness discrepancy prohibits flight under §91.7 is correct
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §91.7 — Airworthiness certificate requirement
14 CFR §43.9 — Maintenance records
CAROL record ERA26FA179 — Cessna 401B N122AT fatal partial power loss (Part 91)

provenance.source: CAROL case ERA26FA179 (Cessna 401B N122AT, Part 91, fatal partial power loss following powerplant servicing). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey ERA26FA179. Accessed 2026-05-17.
